claims.claim: The SEI membrane begins to decompose at 80–120 °C, releasing active lithium for an oxidative reaction with the electrolyte.
claims.quote: When the temperature reaches 80–120 °C, the SEI membrane on the negative electrode begins to decompose, releasing active lithium for an oxidative reaction with the electrolyte.

claims.claim: At 150–200 °C, the positive electrode material releases oxygen, which reacts violently with the electrolyte, significantly increasing the rate of heat production.
claims.quote: As the temperature further increases to 150–200 °C, the positive electrode material releases oxygen, which reacts violently with the electrolyte, significantly increasing the rate of heat production.

claims.claim: Once the temperature exceeds 200 °C, the electrolyte decomposes and the polyolefin diaphragm melts and shrinks, causing direct contact between the positive and negative electrodes.
claims.quote: Once the temperature exceeds 200 °C, the electrolyte decomposes, and the polyolefin diaphragm melts and shrinks, causing direct contact between the positive and negative electrodes.

claims.claim: The melting points of PP and PE separators are around 165 °C and 130 °C respectively, while a PP/PE/PP composite separator melts at around 170 °C and a ceramic-coated separator at around 200 °C.
claims.quote: The melting points of commonly used PP and PE separators are around 165 °C and 130 °C, respectively, while the melting point of a PP/PE/PP composite separator is around 170 °C, and that of a separator with a ceramic coating is around 200 °C.

claims.claim: When the SOC exceeded 60% in three-point bending tests on cylindrical LIBs, the battery experienced significant thermal runaway events such as explosions and fire.
claims.quote: Their results showed that when the SOC exceeded 60%, the battery experienced significant thermal runaway events, such as explosions and fire.

claims.claim: When temperature exceeds 260 °C, fluorinated binders react with intercalated lithium in the negative electrode to produce H2.
claims.quote: When the temperature exceeds 260 °C, fluorinated binders react with the intercalated lithium in the negative electrode to produce H2.

claims.claim: Under overcharging conditions, lithium dendrite deposition occurs on the negative electrode and can penetrate the diaphragm, while excessive lithium delithiation on the positive electrode causes structural collapse and increased oxygen release.
claims.quote: Under overcharging conditions, lithium dendrite deposition occurs on the negative electrode, penetrating the diaphragm, while excessive lithium delithiation on the positive electrode causes structural collapse and increased oxygen release.

claims.claim: Thermal insulation materials can significantly delay the propagation speed of thermal runaway within battery modules.
claims.quote: Hu et al. experimentally examined the effect of thermal insulation materials on thermal runaway propagation and found that such materials can significantly delay the propagation speed.

claims.claim: In a compression test of commercial ternary/graphite Li-ion batteries, indentation enhanced cycling performance when compressed from 4 mm to 6.8 mm over 620 cycles, but the effect became detrimental when indentation depth exceeded 6.5 mm.
claims.quote: The test cells were compressed from 4 mm to 6.8 mm and cycled 620 times. The results indicated that indentation enhanced the cycling performance of the test cells, although the effect became detrimental when the indentation depth exceeded 6.5 mm.

claims.claim: A 1 mm thick phase change material effectively reduced battery temperature under high discharge conditions.
claims.quote: MoraliU demonstrated that the thickness of the phase change material significantly affects the thermal management system's performance, with a 1 mm thick material effectively reducing battery temperature under high discharge conditions.

claims.claim: At a discharge rate of 25C, thermal runaway occurred and the maximum temperature exceeded 500°C.
claims.quote: Especially in the 25C rate condition, the maximum temperature increased exceeds 500 ◦C.

claims.claim: Under the same C-rate conditions, batteries with lower SOC exhibited a faster surface temperature rise rate, while batteries with higher SOC reached a higher maximum surface temperature.
claims.quote: The surface temperature rise rate of batteries with low SOC were faster, and the maximum surface temperature rise of batteries with high SOC were higher.

claims.claim: At 20C discharge rate, the 50% SOC battery (battery 3) had average temperature rise rates of 2.21°C/s, 2.70°C/s, and 4.42°C/s at T1, T2, and T3 respectively, with maximum temperature rises of 100.17°C, 118.40°C, and 128.99°C.
claims.quote: the average temperature rise rate of battery 3 with the state of charge (SOC) of 50 % at T1, T2, and T3 were 2.21◦C/s, 2.70◦C/s, and 4.42◦C/s, respectively, and the maximum temperature rise were 100.17◦C, 118.40◦C, and 128.99◦C respectively.

claims.claim: At 20C discharge rate, the 100% SOC battery (battery 8) had lower average temperature rise rates (1.35°C/s, 1.46°C/s, 3.03°C/s) but higher maximum temperature rise at T3 (156.50°C) compared to the 50% SOC battery at the same rate.
claims.quote: the average temperature rise rate of battery 8 with a state of charge of 100 % at T1, T2, and T3 were 1.35 ◦C/s, 1.46 ◦C/s, and 3.03 ◦C/s, respectively, and the maximum temperature rise were 91.96◦C, 110.08◦C, 156.50◦C.

claims.claim: In the same C-rate and SOC conditions, the negative electrode (T3) had the highest temperature during short circuit, followed by the center (T2), and the positive electrode (T1) had the lowest temperature.
claims.quote: It indicated that the negative electrode of the battery has the highest temperature during the short-circuit process, followed by the center temperature and finally the positive electrode temperature.

claims.claim: Separator shrinkage and tearing were identified as the main causes of jelly roll deformation in batteries subjected to external short circuit.
claims.quote: It is concluded that shrinkage and tearing of the separator were the main causes of the deformation of the battery jelly roll.

claims.claim: When the separator temperature reached 130°C, the micropores of the separator closed and irreversibly blocked, and at 150°C the separator appeared visibly wrinkled and destroyed completely.
claims.quote: when the temperature increased to 130◦C, the internal temperature reached the melting point of the polymer and the micropores of the separator closed and irreversibly blocked the pores of the separator. When the temperature was further increased to 150◦C, the separator appeared to be visibly wrinkled and destroyed completely.

claims.claim: During external short circuit at 100% SOC, the voltage curve showed a rapid initial drop followed by a distinct voltage platform near 2.75 V, caused by the high temperature triggering separator pore closure which hindered Li+ transport.
claims.quote: The presence of a distinct voltage platform in the high SOC group was caused by the high temperature during the external short circuit of the battery that triggered the separator to close its holes, thus hindered the transport of Li+.

claims.claim: Discharge capacity of short-circuit batteries decreased linearly with increasing short-circuit current, with the 100% SOC battery discharge capacity being slightly higher than the 50% SOC battery.
claims.quote: the discharge capacity of the 100 % SOC short-circuit battery was slightly higher than that of the 50 % SOC short-circuit battery, which decreased linearly with the increase of the short-circuit currents.

claims.claim: Over-discharge during the short circuit process caused copper from the negative electrode current collector to oxidize, migrate across the separator, and deposit as Cu metal on the positive electrode surface.
claims.quote: the negative electrode current collector copper foil was oxidized to Cu+ and Cu2+ at high voltage. At the same time, the positive voltage decreased, and these copper ions would be migrated from the negative electrode across the separator to the surface of the positive electrode under the action of the electro-potential difference, and be reduced to Cu+ and Cu metal at the positive electrode, which formed the Cu deposition.

claims.claim: At the triggering temperature T2, the internal temperature of the NCA||Gr battery was 438.3°C while the surface temperature measured 179.9°C, a difference of 258.4°C.
claims.quote: The T2 value corresponding to the surface temperature is 179.9°C, at the same time, the internal temperature has reached 438.3°C, with a difference of 258.4°C.

claims.claim: Using only surface temperature measurement causes incorrect attribution of internal short circuit timing, leading to the false conclusion that internal short circuit occurs after T2 and does not contribute to thermal runaway triggering.
claims.quote: If only the surface temperature is considered in the analysis of thermal runaway behavior, it will be wrongly believed that the internal short circuit occurs after T2 and does not contribute to the thermal runaway triggering, which directly affects the understanding of thermal runaway mechanism and the judgment of whether the internal short circuit is used as the input heat source of the model.

claims.claim: In the self-heating stage (T1 to T2) of NCA||Gr battery thermal runaway, the An+Ele system contributes 53.8% of heat, internal short circuit contributes 27.5%, and the Ca+An system contributes 18.7%.
claims.quote: An+Ele system contributes 53.8% of the heat for thermal runaway triggering, internal short circuit 27.5%, and Ca + An system 18.7%.

claims.claim: The heat generated by internal short circuit is released instantaneously and directly triggers thermal runaway, causing the battery temperature rate to reach 1°C/s.
claims.quote: The heat generated by the internal short circuit is released instantly, making the battery temperature rate reach 1°C s−1. After the thermal runaway is triggered, the reaction between decomposition products of LiPF6 and anode, the reaction of electrolyte solvent with intercalated lithium and the reaction of electrolyte solvent with cathode oxygen release all contribute more heat to make the battery temperature reach the maximum.

claims.claim: After thermal runaway is triggered (T2 to T3), the An+Ele system contributes 59.1%, the Ca+An system contributes 35.6%, and internal short circuit contributes only 5.4% to the temperature rise.
claims.quote: After thermal runaway triggering(T2 ~ T3), QCEI is not contributing, the descending order of heat contribution is: QAn+E > QO2+An > QLi+ES > QCa+B > Qec. An+Ele system contributes 59.1%, internal short circuit 5.4% and Ca + An system 35.6% to the temperature rise after thermal runaway triggering.
